comment_text.sn8.1:1.2: This chapter features the poetry of Vaṅgīsa, the finest poet of the Sangha, providing a prose setting for his Theragāthā verses at Thag 21.1. There are notable differences between the two versions. In several passages, including this one, he reflects on the struggles he faced in his path, showing a high degree of self-awareness and persistence.
comment_text.sn8.1:1.4: Period art depicts women as bare-breasted and draped in elaborate jewellery.
comment_text.sn8.1:1.5: “Dissatisfaction” (anabhirati) with his life of seclusion and celibacy.
comment_text.sn8.1:3.1: There is some confusion regarding the Pali ugga (“warrior chief”). Pali commentaries and Sanskrit sources (Manu 10.9) speak of a caste called ugga (Sanskrit ugra), born of a khattiya father and a sudda mother. They were reviled as cruel, and their duty was to catch snakes and other underground vermin (Manu 10.49). This is not the aristocratic warriors referred to here. The distinction is noted in Medhātithi’s commentary to Manu 4.212, who says the Vedic ugra was a key figure in the king’s alliance, which is supported by the use at DN 3:2.7.2.

root_text.sn8.1:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ āyasmā vaṅgīso āḷaviyaṁ viharati aggāḷave cetiye āyasmatā nigrodhakappena upajjhāyena saddhiṁ.
root_text.sn8.1:1.3: Tena kho pana samayena āyasmā vaṅgīso navako hoti acirapabbajito ohiyyako vihārapālo.
root_text.sn8.1:1.4: Atha kho sambahulā itthiyo samalaṅkaritvā yena aggāḷavako ārāmo tenupasaṅkamiṁsu vihārapekkhikāyo.
root_text.sn8.1:1.5: Atha kho āyasmato vaṅgīsassa tā itthiyo disvā anabhirati uppajjati, rāgo cittaṁ anuddhaṁseti.
root_text.sn8.1:1.7: “alābhā vata me, na vata me lābhā; dulladdhaṁ vata me, na vata me suladdhaṁ;
root_text.sn8.1:1.8: yassa me anabhirati uppannā, rāgo cittaṁ anuddhaṁseti, taṁ kutettha labbhā, yaṁ me paro anabhiratiṁ vinodetvā abhiratiṁ uppādeyya.
root_text.sn8.1:1.9: Yannūnāhaṁ attanāva attano anabhiratiṁ vinodetvā abhiratiṁ uppādeyyan”ti.
root_text.sn8.1:1.10: Atha kho āyasmā vaṅgīso attanāva attano anabhiratiṁ vinodetvā abhiratiṁ uppādetvā tāyaṁ velāyaṁ imā gāthāyo abhāsi:

translation_text.sn8.1:1.2: At one time Venerable Vaṅgīsa was staying near Āḷavī, at Āḷavī’s premier shrine, together with his mentor, Venerable Nigrodhakappa.
translation_text.sn8.1:1.3: Now at that time Vaṅgīsa was junior, recently gone forth. He had been left behind to look after the dwelling.
translation_text.sn8.1:1.4: Then several women dressed in all their finery went to the monastery at Āḷavī’s premier shrine in order to inspect the dwelling.
translation_text.sn8.1:1.5: When Vaṅgīsa saw them he became dissatisfied, with lust infecting his mind.
translation_text.sn8.1:1.8: that I’ve become dissatisfied, with lust infecting my mind. How is it possible for someone else to dispel my discontent and give rise to satisfaction?
translation_text.sn8.1:1.9: Why don’t I do it myself?”
translation_text.sn8.1:1.10: Then, on the occasion of dispelling his own discontent and giving rise to satisfaction, he recited these verses:
translation_text.sn8.1:2.4: by the rude thoughts of the Dark One.
translation_text.sn8.1:3.1: Even if a thousand warrior chiefs and great archers,
translation_text.sn8.1:4.4: for I stand firm in the teaching.
translation_text.sn8.1:5.1: I heard with my own ears
translation_text.sn8.1:5.2: from the Buddha, Kinsman of the Sun,
translation_text.sn8.1:5.3: about the path going to extinguishment;
translation_text.sn8.1:6.1: Wicked One, if you come near me
translation_text.sn8.1:6.3: I’ll make sure that you, Death,
translation_text.sn8.1:6.4: won’t even see the path I take.”

variant_text.sn8.1:4.1: etato → ettato (bj, pts1ed, pts2ed, mr); ettako (bj); ettakā (sya-all, km)
